It's getting on for midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"The growth of the soul takes place through many incarnations, and different attainments are required of it at different stages of its development, therefore no objective standard of achievement is set; a principle is taught, an ideal is held, and each student is counseled to apply that principle and follow that ideal in the circumstances in which he finds himself, for it is only as he proves faithful over few things that he will be entrusted with many." 

That's from Dion Fortune's The Training and Work of the Initiate, one of the books of hers that did an enormous amount to shape my understanding of magic and the occult.   

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will probably be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- as in, attempts to ask new questions will be deleted. See you next week!***

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

replies.title: Re: Mobile phones and meditation

For what it's worth I use a phone or a laptop for a meditation timer. Since my laptop is an apple I use iTunes but any platform and a music player application will do the same thing. I looked online and found bells that I like: a gong to begin, a high chime as a five minute reminder and a triple bell to end. In between I put the amount of silence that I want. Note; you can find silence online as a file. I just put these together, bell, silence, bell, silence, etc. and play it like a long song with iTunes.

The best thing is that after I do the SOP I'm not in the mood to deal with timers and such so I just touch the space bar on the computer and in 30 seconds, which gives me time to sit and get in position, the first bell rings. After the three bells at the end I can just walk away without having to close anything on the computer. Thus I do not have to break away from any thoughts or sensations that I consider significant just to deal with the physical act of timing my meditation.

I don't like screen savers and moving light so I cover the screen with a piece of cardboard cut for that purpose. This always elicits a laugh from my younger friends. With the phone I just place it face down. Same effect.

This is much easier than it sounds. Furthermore, if you want to change the timing in any way it's quite easy.....or just do a second or third meditation set up if there are different ways that you want to approach your daily activity. If you have any problem setting it up find a youngster to help you. Seems they can do this stuff with the greatest of ease. And note, I am not very computer literate but an hour or two of set up has served me well for the last year and a half.

Best wishes

comments.content:
JMG last week I ask how to avoid diabetes by changing my self-indulgent character. You counseled learning self-discipline and becoming acquainted with discomfort. That was good advice, thanks. As I thought about that this week I wondered how self-discipline relates to will power. Are they the same or different? replies.title: Re: Self-discipline vs willpower
replies.content: Self-discipline is one of the things you do with willpower. Willpower is like electricity; self-discipline is like a toaster oven -- one of the things it can power.

replies.title: Re: Self-discipline vs willpower
replies.content: Thank you JMG that make sense

replies.title: Re: Self-discipline vs willpower
replies.content:
Goats and Roses

This question/answer has been like a little explosion in my mind. I have always thought of will and self-discipline as being synonymous. Now that I can hold them in my mind, unstuck from one another, I feel like I have a very vague understanding of will. Could you comment generally about what will is? And what other "toaster ovens" are powered by will? Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Self-discipline vs willpower
replies.content:
The best way to understand will is to experience it. Right now, reach out with one of your hands and touch something -- it doesn't matter what. Now do it again, slowly, paying close attention to what you experience. Will is what sets your arm and hand in motion. ("It is by will alone that the hand moves...") Every intentional act you perform is set in motion by your will, and will can be described as the capacity to perform intentional actions. Self-discipline is an intentional action, or more precisely a whole series of intentional actions. Everything else that you choose to do is another intentional action, and your habits are also intentional actions that have become relatively fixed and repetitive.

What this means, of course, is that you have all the willpower you could possibly need -- but you may not know how to use it yet. The thing that gums up the works is when you will two contradictory things at once -- and most of us do that all the time, without realizing it. Learning to will one thing at a time is one of the basic skills of the mage.

replies.title: Re: Self-discipline vs willpower
replies.content:
Not the OP: Could I trouble you to say a little more on the last point, how to avoid willing two things at once? Obviously, meditation is needed and will be done, but this is certainly a habit of mine. In my case, it's an issue with limits - I'd like to be a great musician and a studious mage, but I can't do both at once, especially with a full-time job.

So here time and energy become the limiting factors, unless other activities can be shed in order to free those things up. But then again, there have been times where I've actually had both time and energy, and have tried to practice both things, but it still doesn't seem that effective to do both things at once (ie, right after one another in time). Now I am just learning magic, I've left the music aside, and it is far more effective in terms of what I'm learning, but I can't explain why... I'd welcome any comments you may have!

Are there other common situations where this issue emerges? Are there signs to recognize it when it occurs?

replies.title: Hekate

Certainly not based on a TV show mention, lol. Though I've enjoyed "Sabrina" since it came out. I did quite a bit of research and reading at first.

At 63 this week, I can't speak for those younger but this goddess definitely has a following in the witch community. Some of it might be just fad attraction but Hekate does have a history which stretches back pre-Greek and Roman myth. Its told that even Zeus honored her as a powerful ally outside of the Greek pantheon. Her role as a guardian of the underworld and a protector of those who walk the darker paths probably has a lot to do with that following as well.

Some of the appeal among the new converts may be that she isn't traditionally a warrior goddess. I believe she started as a hearth goddess and moved up the ladder in duties and influence as her following spread out of Asia Minor and more into the civilized world of Mediterranean of first Persian, then Greek and Roman cultures.

There is certainly enough youtube videos by her self proclaimed priests and priestesses out there. How shallow or deep is her devotion I'm not sure, but I get the impression the number of people who call her a patron is significant.

My experience so far, is she isn't a goddess that comes easily to your call. She's definitely also one to hold a high standard of your behavior. I've had both encouragement and dissatisfaction with my own actions so far.

Whether she becomes a player in the post-collapse world will be something I'm too old to witness. Might be something to include in a Post Oil story or look for in the next reincarnation.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
I have a question about a geomantic chart regarding a piece of land my partner and I are planning to purchase to build a home on. On many levels, the land has a magical draw, for intuitive as well as practical reasons. We put in a conditional offer last month which was accepted, and need to finalize the deal this Wednesday if we want to go forward.
Here is the chart I cast for the question: should we move forward with the purchase of this land:

First mother : Coch
Second mother: cyswllt
Third mother: fford
Fourth mother: Fford

First daughter: bendith fawr
Second daughter: fford
Third daughter: pen y ddraig
Fourth daughter: bending fawr

First niece: gwyn
Second niece: pobl

Third niece: bendith fach
Fourth niece: coch

Right witness: gwyn
Left witness: llawenydd

Judge: Colled

Overall I feel good about the chart, but don’t want to overlook the fact that the judge is not favourable. How would you interpret colled in this context? One reality is that this is definitely stretching us financially - there is cheaper land in the same area, but we have a feeling of rightness and affinity with this land In a number of ways that would be long to get into.

I’m inclined to read this chart as: So long as you are wise and prudent in how you go about your decisions (Gwyn) (I.e. being prudent with building costs) you will be happy with this choice (llawenydd), but it’s going to cost you. How does that sound to you? In spite of some trepidation and a certain number of bumps in the road thus far, when we connect with our sense of purpose on a grounded level, we want to go ahead with the land, but I don’t want to be sugar coating a negative reading to justify try at decision. So if you think I’m doin go that, by all means say so!

I feel encouraged by Bendirh Fawr in the third and fourth triplicates, representing home and spouse specifically (success after a delay, success through effort and persistence, which both feel very realistic).

The way of points leading back to Fford I’m not sure what to make of. That this will involve travel (it is a significant geographical move)? Representing movement as in we should go for it? Or, representing that we should let this go and move on?

I’m also mindful of your advice to avoid making major life decisions on the basis of a single divination as a beginner, which I am. For that reason I have opted not to do divination regarding this at all over the several months we have been working on this, and sticking to other modes of decision making. But today I had a feeling to go for it. If you (or anyone else) has suggestions for other questions to explore different aspects of this issue, I’m open to that, too. Thanks!

The Way of Points indicates what's behind the question -- in this case, it's a referelce to the move, but also suggests to me that you've come a long way to the point at which this is an option.

comments.title: a conjunction of synchronicities
comments.content:
One of my goals for our rolling lock-down was to refresh my Russian language skills. That was proceeding apace until I saw a guy with both his arms in casts at the local supermarket. Since I have had multiple wrist breaks, four wrist surgeries, and four shoulder surgeries, including having my right wrist in a cast and my left arm in a sling at the same time, I had some idea what he must be going through. So I went over and asked what had happened.

He could barely explain in English that he had come from Belarus in February to work, got locked-down, and then fell off a ladder once the lock-down eased. I asked some questions in Russian and realized that he knew no Russian speakers in our neighborhood. So we started going for long walks every day, and I started carrying his groceries and taking out his trash. I was his moving crew when he found a cheaper apartment with other Russian speakers; then I drove him around his new neighborhood to get his bearings. His English and my Russian are improving rapidly, and his wrists are recovering quite well.

As a competitive athlete and coach, he was very worried about how his wrists would recover. I know quite a bit about all sorts of hand and wrist injuries after two full years of asking questions at occupational therapy. So I could explain to him that the casts would get replaced with splints and that a physical therapist would then give him exercises. On our walks I always asked which of his fingers he could touch with his thumbs, and other little reassuring victories. Now that he is going to therapy, he will only do his partnered exercises with me because I've gone through the same process so many times.

I certainly wasn't praying for a bewildered Russian speaker to show up with both his wrists broken, but that's what the universe provided. I also wasn't feeling any particular urgency about retiring the karmic debt I had accumulated from so many people helping me both when I was incapacitated and when I first moved to Indonesia speaking very little Indonesian. I hadn't even realized that with so many people adamantly social distancing, I was missing just being able to lend a helping hand. The gods are so much better at recognizing our needs and weaving together synchronicities that we couldn't ever hope to come up with.

In some ways my life has had some wonderful synchronicities, but never this easy. While it's nice hanging out and telling jokes in the back yard with Ukrainians and Belarusians, what on earth is going on? It feels like the resistance to things working out well is just not there like it used to be. Like a great disturbance in the Force has resolved, as if millions of voices suddenly cried out in relief, and were suddenly freed. Has the energy that the ruling class used to put into making everything go awry, now been diverted into desperately trying to hold together their crumbling status quo?

It really feels like less energy is needed to try new things. And that I can follow through on the things that promote life with less effort than before. Of course, the panic police are still Karening and cancelling their way to nirvana, but they have sort of faded into the background noise here. Perhaps that's all they ever were. Is anyone else feeling relieved of a depleting burden? Does it feel like life is being allowed to flow along its natural course again, at least for a brief while? Like the teacher has stepped out the room, and we can show real interest for once in something other than the prescribed lesson?

—Christophe

replies.title: Re: a conjunction of synchronicities
replies.content: Yes, I do feel relieved of the depleting burden that had begun with the COVID lockdowns and extended through June. I'm also experiencing some synchronicities that I am enjoying.

replies.title: Re: a conjunction of synchronicities
replies.content: Hmm! Fascinating. I'd noticed a general brightening and clarifying of the energies recently, but I'd assumed it was just me -- I've been doing some fairly intensive spiritual work. If it's more general, that's a very good sign.

comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I was mulling over asking you this question, and then lo and behold you select a quotation this week which is directly relevant to it. Must be a sign, I said to myself.
I've been interested for a long time in the idea of Non-duality, which for those who haven't encountered it, very briefly, is a "consciousness only" model of the universe, in which we are simply temporary, limited implementations of a universal consciousness. It has its origins in Eastern teaching, although there are Western writers (Meister Eckhart, for example) who say similar things. Instead of the classic distinction between the world "out there" and the world of consciousness, it proposes that the world is "not two", (ad-vaita in the original Sanskrit) and all is consciousness. Time and space then, are human constructs rather than existing independently.
I've been wondering how, if at all, this could be reconciled with the theoretical frameworks of magic. Your own writings (eg Mystery Teachings from the Living Earth) seem to take a very differ tack, and of course the idea of reincarnation itself, as in today's quotation, isn't immediately compatible with the non-existence of time. On the other hand, modern non-duality teachers like Rupert Spira emphasise that what we perceive in the world has to be treated as real, and has consequences, just as the things we perceive in the dream-state have reality there.
So I was fascinated to see, following one of your recommendations, that Mouni Sadhu, in his book Ways to Self-Realisation, talks a great deal about the teachings of Ramana Maharshi, the most distinguished modern figure on non-dual teaching. He, at least, appears to think that non-duality is compatible with magical practice, or to be more precise the theory behind it.
What do you think?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm not JMG, but I'm working in both a western occult track and a self-inquiry/non-duality track, though most of the contemporary neo-advaita teachers (including Spira) leave me unimpressed (of course, there are also many contemporary occultists who likewise leave me unimpressed.) Reconciling the differences has been an interesting part of my practice, but one teaching that has really helped is that of Rob Burbea and his take on emptiness (Seeing That Frees is his great book, but he has many hours of audios on "Imaginal" practice that basically fuses Dharma practice with Jung/Corbin and ends up with something like scrying and diety work...) but anyway, the basic teaching is that there are many ways to view the world, and each view is inherently empty, but even the view that every view is empty is empty. Seeing that on a deep level frees one to utilize different views without being caught up in any one of them... all views are true in a way, and ultimately false in that they only glimpse a fragment of Reality, if any amount of Reality at all. When it comes down to it, many teachers say that Self-Realization can't really be talked about, and can only be experienced directly, and until that happens, conceptual frameworks of a nondual universe can hinder as much as help. As Fortune says, these teachings are "to train the mind, not inform it."

Btw, this site was made by a friend and includes reviews of some of the "nondual" teachers I find more useful, including some personal mentors: http://www.spiritualteachers.org/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Most occult teachings take nonduality as their basis, but they also make the same point that Rupert Spira did; time, space, and individual objects may not be real in any ultimate sense, but while we're incarnate and at this level of consciousness, they certainly behave as though they're real to us!  Magic exists in the world of appearances, and it shapes those appearances, including the appearance we each call "me." As for reincarnation, we don't get a free pass out of the human level of consciousness just by dying, you know, so time -- be it ever so illusory from the point of view of the Absolute -- continues to exist for us, as human beings, incarnate or discarnate.

comments.title: Natural Materials and Ritual

replies.title: Re: Natural Materials and Ritual
replies.content:

If you want one never to speak to you again, hand him a copy of John McClenon's Deviant Science: The Case of Parapsychology, which documents (via a controlled double-blind study!) that a large majority of people in the sciences who dismiss parapsychology are motivated by a priori arguments based on dogmatic belief in materialism, while the vast majority of people in the sciences who accept the possibility of psychic phenomena do so because they've experienced something genuinely weird.

replies.title: Re: Natural Materials and Ritual
replies.content: 1) A decade ago, there about, I was a very strong rational materalist, had most the best arguments for that philosophy down pat. I can say that there were three philosophic pieces that cracked my shell at that time. The first was reading Spinoza's ethics; I wanted to write a paper arguing that he was effectively an atheist, but trying to do so made me realize that I didn't have that solid a definition of what constitutes a God. I wouldn't recommend this for most, but its good grist for the mill, that being said Spinoza didn't leave that deep an impression on me otherwise in the long run. The most important study I did to open my mind was of Gregory Bateson's 'Steps to an Ecology of Mind' which I recommend to anyone, its a hecking good read. What's great about Bateson is that he is very much a rationalist, but his thinking makes room for a fair degree of other phenomena. I think I got into the Archdruid Report back when largely because JMG referenced Bateson a few times, and it made me realize that Bateson's philosophy could easily be extended to allow much further than I at first guessed. This book is great to offer to a rationalist because its not that threatening, but leaves the door cracked. The simplest thing that effected me to opening up to more thing between Heaven and Earth than I had dremt in my metaphysics was reading some quotes online from https://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Sidney_Morgenbesser Sidney Morgenbesser. The guy is a decent philosopher, and a hecking funny guy. the following line effected me very deeply: Morgenbesser in response to B.F. Skinner: "Are you telling me it's wrong to anthropomorphize people?" This had so powerful an effect on me because I realized that most of my strongest arguments against theism were just about as effective as arguments against the anthropomorphizing people, and that if I accept it as sensible to treat humans (with our well explored material basis) as persons that its not too much crazier to go further.

comments.title: The O.E. Library Critic
comments.content:
Henry Newlin Stokes' The O.E. Library Critic published a tongue-and-cheek essay which purportedly claimed that two trained clairvoyants had looked into the future to around the year 2500 and had in turn reported their findings. The state of civilization had drastically changed!

"Those buildings which look like temples are public schools. It is thought that children should be reared in beautiful surroundings and that a temple on six days in the week is better than just on Sundays. In spring the schools adjourn to the woods and fields for nature studies and boy-scouting; consequently truancy is unknown. School superintendents who persist in cramming arithmetic and grammar in spring weather are confined in damp cellars and are fed on bread and water, with beer and pretzels on Sundays. Much attention is paid to public architecture. Public statues, except of an ideal kind, are not in vogue, because it is recognized that the pride of the nation is in its living; that the dead will soon return, and that no man wants to come back to find that his fellow citizens have expressed their approval by placing him astride a bronze horse with the blind staggers.

The struggle for existence is still at the front, under the name Karmic Education. It has come to be recognized that life is a school not a prison. Consequently the young are put to such work as will best develop their latent faculties rather than make machines of them. In this the parents have the aid of clairvoyants and astrologers. It is admitted, of course, that one must not be employed for that for which he is totally unfitted, and to indemnify both employers and employees who suffer from the mistakes of seers and star experts, there is a National Insurance against Fools."

excerpt from April 9, 1913

replies.title: Re: Taking the First Result of Magic (or Not)
replies.content: I started teaching people to do that because I encountered a whole series of people who would do a ritual to get something, and it would show up, and they'd say, "Yes, but that's not exactly what I wanted" -- and then they'd keep on saying the same thing to every other thing that turned up, because they didn't actually want the thing they were doing the ritual to get. Saying "Take the first thing that shows up" was an effective way to short-circuit that self-defeating habit -- not least because people wouldn't waste their time and mine on a ritual if they didn't actually want the results of it. If you've got enough self-knowledge and self-mastery that you don't have to worry about that trap, sure, you can pick and choose among the results.

comments.title: Knowing what plane you are on
comments.content:
In the discussion on the Cosmic Doc, you mentioned that the Core Shamans were stuck betwixt and between the earth and spiritual planes. How does someone know that and how do they move?

How do you know what plane you are on? Are there signs. And how do you keep your balance between the two?

Lilac Anthropoid Weasel (Neptune's Dolphins)

replies.title: Re: Knowing what plane you are on
replies.content:
Well, you need to have a good sense of what the planes and sub-planes are, and that will give you a pretty good basis for judging. For example, in Dion Fortune's taxonomy of the planes, most people have their consciousness centered on the lower astral, upper astral, or lower mental planes. The lower astral has for its keynote passions and biological drives; the upper astral, emotions and concrete mental images; the lower mental, ideals and abstract concepts. So you would observe yourself and notice what motivates you, what you spend most of your time thinking about, what appeals to you, and so on. None of these are better than any of the others, btw; we all have to experience the world through every state of consciousness possible to human beings.

As for finding the balance, the best advice I ever got was to pay some attention to all the planes. It's good to spend time just being in the material world, relating to it through the senses; it's good to spend time dealing with one's own inner life on all its various levels; and it's good to relate to the spiritual powers of the planes we haven't reached yet -- that is to say, the gods.

comments.content:
HI John

Well we finally got a visible comet this year. Comet Neowise.

What do you think the astrological implications are for this comet?

replies.title: Re: comet
replies.content:
Went to look this up. Technically, the comet was discovered in late March, but that was a telemetric discovery.

Naked-eye, checking Twitter reports under #neowise, the earliest I can find is July 2, 02:49 UTC (04:49 CEST) in Trecastagni, Sicily, Italy. The comet was reported as being at 2.5 degrees altitude over the horizon, which based on the Ascendant at that moment places the comet's position at naked-eye sighting at approximately 25 degrees 10 minutes of Gemini.

That could be subject to the effects of atmospheric refraction, though, or to the comet not being right on the ecliptic (its declination is 27 degrees 52 minutes) - the ephemeris had it at 5h 56m 48s of right ascension at that moment, which corresponds to about 29 degrees 12 minutes Gemini.

Source: https://twitter.com/vivstoitsis/status/1278599260951293958

In all pictures I've seen of the comet, it appears yellow-orange in color, but I'm not sure how much of that is its real color and how much is the glare of sunrise.

replies.title: Re: comet
replies.content: I just erected a chart for the sighting. A comet in a mutable sign, according to Raphael, indicates pestilence and sedition; rising in the east, it signifies the advent of a great lawgiver. I'll have to do some digging in the older literature to get more details. Thank you for this!

replies.title: Re: comet
replies.content: Right now the U.S. has both pestilence and sedition.  Wow!

replies.title: Re: comet
replies.content: And thank you for this!

replies.title: Re: comet
replies.content:
Sidereal Taurus, tropical Gemini.

Right Ascension is measured such that 0h 00m 00s is the boreal vernal equinox point, i.e. 0 degrees 0 minutes 0 seconds tropical Aries. From there it's precisely 2 hours of RA to the next sign. So:

0:00:00 - 1:59:59.999... = tropical Aries
2:00:00 - 3:59:59.999... = tropical Taurus
4:00:00 - 5:59:59.999... = tropical Gemini, etc., up to
22:00:00 - 23:59:59.999... = tropical Pisces.

(You can kind of see this on the later dates of that ephemeris. The sudden spike in right ascension is the perihelion whipsaw about the sun, which we know to be in tropical Cancer at this time of year :) )

Four minutes of right ascension equate to one degree on the zodiac, and four seconds of right ascension to one minute on the zodiac.

comments.title: Friskiness during SOP?
comments.content:
I've (finally!) started a daily SOP practice, which is going very well. I'd like to ask about a slightly weird physiological/energetic effect I'm experiencing:

During the closing, while visualizing the sphere of white energy expanding out from my solar plexus, I'm finding myself very naturally falling into a breathing rhythm in which I feel energy flowing from above and below into me during my in-breath and then rushing outward to fill and push out the sphere on the out-breath. With each breath cycle I move another "breath" of energy through me outward and push the sphere a bit larger. This gets more and more extreme as I continue, not in any very frightening way, but very powerfully, until I reach my current stopping point (to include the entirety of the small house in which my family now lives, but not beyond). It gets strong enough to lift my arms away from my body on the out-breath and sometimes even feels slightly explosive. This is sometimes accompanied by full-body tension spasms, not unpleasant but strong. I am a serious amateur weightlifter, so I'm familiar and comfortable with strong muscular tension-- this phenomenon is powerful enough that if I weren't, I might be a bit frightened.

Is this bad news/okay/good news/totally irrelevant? :)

My nagging worry is that I'm creating a practice in which my eventual progress will be limited by my ability to contain energy like a bagpipes bellows, whereas I would think the energy should be flowing more continuously through me.

Unless I've got the whole thing backwards and upside-down, which wouldn't be all that surprising since I only started last Tuesday! (I'm looking forward to introducing the element of air tomorrow.)

Thanks for any advice or comment, and thanks again for this wonderful island of sanity!

replies.title: Re: Friskiness during SOP?

It's not a problem -- quite the contrary. Let it happen. As you continue with the SoP, you'll find that the energies begin to flow more smoothly.

replies.title: Re: Friskiness during SOP?
replies.content:
Interesting, and thank you very much for the reassurance. I'll stick with it, of course, and if there are any developments that seem worth reporting on I'll bring them back.

Just a little more anecdata right now in case anyone else is experiencing or think they might be experiencing this: It is rather intense, but not at all unpleasant. Today during SOP I noticed that my arms are being rotated (very much what it feels like-- not me moving them, no will involved, they're just responding to what I'm doing with the energies and breath) up until they point almost directly out from my shoulders, maybe a few degrees shy, and on my out-breaths there is a definite and quite powerful sense of energy moving from my solar plexus center up slightly and out via my arms to grow that sphere a bit larger. As that happens my fingers are pushed apart and wriggle a bit-- who knew that "jazz hands" were part of occult ritual? :)

comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I had a rather interesting experience last Thursday evening as I recited (with incense burning) the Orphic Hymn to Jupiter/Jove during the correct planetary hour. I'm not sure if this auspicious or not, but there happened to be a rather manic thunderstorm happening during that hour. Right at the conclusion of my recitation I suddenly felt a huge jolt of energy in my solar plexus and then for about the next half hour I felt like I had downed a Monster energy drink or maybe several cups of coffee; I was quite wide awake and wired despite the fact that prior to performing the ritual I had felt sluggish/tired all day. After that half hour, the effect subsided and I returned to normal and was able to sleep just fine when I went to bed. I laughed a bit and thought to myself, "wow, that was pretty cool. I wonder what actually happened." And then I affectionately named the experience, "the Jovian Jolt."

1. Have you heard of people reporting similar experiences happening under such conditions? Or alternatively, have you experienced anything like that yourself?

2. Might I have gotten the attention of a sky/weather spirit involved with the thunderstorm happening at the time? It seems like the Greeks associated various sky and weather spirits as names falling under the Zeus umbrella.

3. Do you believe there is any validity to the modern occult teaching that each soul has a primary "soul ray" associated with one of the planets that defines its primary mode of being, and that each individual lifetime the soul takes on the superficial persona or "personality ray" of other planets (usually other than the primary one) that clouds the soul's overall experience over many lifetimes? I can't seem to trace this teaching back to much of anything prior to the 2nd wave of Theosophism (which I find to be rather suspect to begin with), so I'm rather skeptical. But there does seem to be something to the idea that each individual soul is fundamentally different according to some specific archetypal influence from the time of the soul's creation.

4. Do you think Buddhism, or at least specific forms of it, will adapt well to the energies of Aquarius and perhaps find itself a niche in the West during the collapse of the Faustian civilization? I've long been thinking about some type of Asian pseudomorphosis filling in the gaps here in the US (before the homegrown American culture matures into a coherent form) as the remaining Faustian forms become increasingly shrill and psychotic as that paradigm loses its grip over the hearts and minds of the American population. Something about Buddhist cosmology and the doctrinal flexibility of some of its schools seems very adaptable to various cultures all over the world.

-Mauve Oracular Weasel

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 4. At present North American Buddhism is very much linked to the privileged left. This will likely be a major problem for it, but if it can detach itself, then I think it'll thrive.

comments.title: Kobo Daishi and Dr Taverner etc
comments.content:
JMG:

1. I recently visited the Koya san mountaintop temple town in Japan. As I’m sure you know, Kobo Daishi established the esoteric Shingon Buddhist sect there 1200 years ago. It’s usually like Disneyland filled with tourists, but there are currently virtually no tourists in Japan, and it was pouring with rain to boot, so my wife and I had the unique experience of wandering around a deserted town with all the shops and many of the temples closed etc (except for the torrential downpour and a stinging headache I had from the altitude).

Anyway, I walked the entire length of the path through the massive cemetery (Okunoin), at the end of which is Kobo Daishi’s mausoleum and had the privilege of being virtually alone. Amazing experience (I only wish I could have seen it lit up with lamps at night but my headache got even worse and I was asleep by dinnertime). Walking through thousand year old tombs was amazing - particularly stopping at a bridge overlooking the tomb site of an ancient Emperor - apparently he had died down the mountain nearby in Kyoto, but “divine beings” had transported him up the mountain to Koyasan where his body landed, and Kobo Daishi (then still alive) had arranged for him to be laid to rest on the holy site I saw. You can just see the clearing from the path and there’s definitely something about it.

But the best part was the Kobo Daishi mausoleum itself (the actual building was closed so I couldn’t go in and see the eternal flame that has been kept burning for over 1000 years) - there’s a last stretch of 50 yards or so where you cross an inner bridge and go into a kind of inner cemetery compound that’s completely surrounded by a river and cut off from the (massive) outer cemetery (there’s a bunch of inner compound graves and the Mausoleum at its heart with the forest and mountains behind it - you’d never know you were in the 21st century there but it’s only a short walk from the main road)

I can’t describe it and I don’t know what it was, but something distinctly changes in the atmosphere when you cross over the inner bridge. I had the privilege of being completely alone there (wife had long gone back to escape the rain) for about 30 minutes as dusk fell and I just soaked in the atmosphere, despite a pounding headache and the rain. No photography is allowed once you cross the inner bridge, not just when you reach the mausoleum - I don’t understand why since it is allowed in the rest of the compound (you also bow again when you cross the inner bridge, just as you do at main entrance bridge to the outer compound 1km away which is supposed to be the end of Kobo Daishi’s domain where his spirit comes to bid farewell to visitors).

There’s actually also a modern section of the outer compound where clearly 20th and 21st century rich people have built tombs and paid a lot of money to be laid to rest there - you can actually see a bunch of corporate logos etc on the tombs.

The outer compound was also quiet - no one was around and everything was closed. But when you walk like 10 metres across the bridge into the inner compound it suddenly becomes *distinctly* quieter - there’s like a hush and all you can hear is the surrounding stream and the rain and some noises from the wood (I walked back and forth a couple of times and it is very obvious).

I am not psychically sensitive but it feels like that inner compound is just waiting..it endures and nothing about it has changed in a thousand years (it has certainly been centuries since the last tombs were built in there although the mausoleum has been modernized and a newer building built next door). Perhaps something something to do with the legend that Kobo Daishi lies waiting and eternally meditating inside the mausoleum for the Buddha Maitreya or the Buddha of the future.

It was not “dead” or deathly or creepy at all - it’s in the middle of a wood with flowing water - but it’s amazing. I just soaked it all up and wished I could have come at night with no rain.

I guess I partly just wrote this to keep the memories fresh in my mind and report on the trip but also to ask - I didn’t imagine the atmosphere or the change in the inner compound did I? What was it, do you think?

2. Continuing with Koya san, the next morning, my wife and I went into one of the few open shops and spotted some tiny Jizo statues (advertised in the shops for tourists as the protector of children but more accurately the protector and patron of babies who were never born or miscarried - he carries their souls to heaven) and my wife and I wanted to buy some but the shop didn’t take cards and we were short of cash.

Then we thought it wasn’t meant to be and walked on to another temple (Kongobu-ji - the head temple of the Shingon sect) and I was about to go in and had an impulse and told my wife “No, it’s important to you to buy Jizo - let’s go and hunt down the only ATM in town and get cash to buy it” - and we did (the shopkeeper was thrilled).

At this point, I should explain that we had left our two kids with friends overnight while we were at Koyasan, and that between child 1 and 2, we had a number of miscarriages and failed IVF attempts. We have accepted it now, and especially we would not have our daughter if not for those, but they were sad at the time.

That night, back with our kids at home, my 5 year old daughter was restless and talking aloud in her sleep (she often does this and often wakes up - very light sleeper) and my wife went into their bedroom to comfort her, and then got an incredible shock when my daughter said (in her sleep while dreaming): “No, no, I’m not talking about the babies that died - I’m talking about me!”

I just…I have no words. I don’t know what to make of it - my wife may have told my daughter that she lost some babies before her (I don’t know), but even if my daughter knew that..the coincidence of her saying that in her sleep the very day we bought the Jizo statues to honour the souls that couldn’t be born with us - just seems a bit too much to bear.

Do you have any idea what could be going on?

3. I also recently read Dion Fortune’s Secrets of Dr Taverner short stories. Her writing skills aren’t quite the match of Arthur Conan Doyle but the Holmes/Watson style framing and the stories themselves are excellent.

Dion Fortune says in the intro that while the stories are not literally true, every single one is based on fact (sometimes multiple real incidents blended), and in fact that more often than not she had to tone *down* the details to make them more believable (and also that Dr Taverner himself was essentially real and was her mentor).

You may not remember the details of the stories themselves but I wanted to ask if I was correct in thinking that most of them are essentially fictionalized accounts of real occult experiences. For example:

a) One character says that she agreed to marry a man even though she didn’t particularly like him, but when she was in his presence she thought he was amazing and happily agreed to marry him but when far away from him she couldn’t understand why she had agreed to it. This sounds to me a lot like how you’ve described the effects of a love spell.

b) In another story, a character who has peripherally observed occult matters is walking through nature and starts to feel as if nature itself is “calling” to him to decide whether he wants to join the occult world or stay in his mundane life and the beauty of the scene around him affects him and he feels like this is a conscious step he must take and then “something” draws him through the barrier and suddenly he feels like he’s at a different level of consciousness and awareness - nothing has changed, but everything has changed and he can feel the web of life all around him. This sounds to me a lot like the initiation experiences you’ve described? Self-initiation?

c) There are others (the vampire story was particularly interesting - I didn’t realize when reading your book Monsters that they could be so clearly visible and take shape as a person) but I guess my overall question is whether you thought the book was a reasonably accurate depiction of occult happenings (with maybe some artistic licence) or if you thought it had been “jazzed up”?

replies.title: Re: Kobo Daishi and Dr Taverner etc
replies.content:
1) No, you didn't imagine it. Many other people have noticed the same thing. If I ever have the chance to go there, I'll look forward to the experience! As to what it is -- that's what holiness feels like.

2) Jizo Bosatsu is also the guardian of living children -- there's a good description of his many roles here. I think you got a message that he's interested in extending his blessing to your living children, as well as to those who were never born. You might consider setting up the statue in an appropriate place in your home, burning some incense, and reciting Jizo's mantra (given in the webpage I linked to).

3) Yes, the real "Dr. Taverner" was a Theosophist named Theodore Moriarty, who was Fortune's teacher. I've never been sure how to take Fortune's claim that the stories had to be toned down -- myself, I think they were jazzed up a little. That said, I've experienced things very like the first two bits you cite; the scenes in my novels where characters have to fight off attempts to control their thinking are inspired by events like the first one.

replies.title: Re: Kobo Daishi and Dr Taverner etc
replies.content:
(OP Here)

Thank you JMG!

1. I hadn't made this connection till just now but your phrase "that's what holiness feels like" just triggered some memories.

My background is Hindu and I grew up walking distance from this ancient Hindu temple in India (800 or 1400 years old, the history is unclear) and my father often took me there.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kapaleeshwarar_Temple

It's anything but quiet - always full of people and the bustle of worship. But there's a feeling inside - kind of an internal calmness. I can't put words to it (until now I guess!), but I always enjoyed being inside the main courtyard because of an indefinable "feeling".

I also remember going into the Sacre Coeur Basilica in Paris, many years ago as a teenage backpacker. It was late on a Sunday evening I think and very quiet and a service was going on. I slipped into the back for a few minutes and listened and came out feeling recharged and happy and touched - as if I'd just had a special experience. The memory of this isn't as clear as Koyasan or the above temple but I distinctly remember coming out and feeling "that was an amazing experience - I love this place and how it makes me feel". I've never been back but it remains my favourite place in Paris.

I guess I'm writing all this because it helps me figure out that all those experiences are variations of the same thing - "that's what holiness feels like". Once you can feel the "flavour" of it - and I think I have it now - it should be possible to sense it again.

It's also interesting that holy places of three different religions can inspire very similar feelings (although come to think of it, I *didn't* get that feeling when I was in St Paul's Cathedral in London - I just wandered around reading about the history of the place).

2. Thank you re Jizo Bosatsu - that makes perfect sense and I will do so.

3. Dion Fortune's Psychic Self-Defence book also had an anecdote about being psychically trapped in a nursing home where she worked and how hard it was for her to escape. I don't know much about the occult (I've been reading your books and Dion Fortune's and have read Magic Monday for a couple of years), but based just on that, the Dr Taverner stories seem broadly plausible, albeit a bit "tightened up for dramatic effect" like they do in movies.

The one story that I struggle to believe is the vampire story, and yet that is the one Fortune singles out in the introduction and says something like "every word of this one is true".

replies.title: Re: Sane Occultism by Dion Fortune

comments.title: Possible Psychism in Child
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I had an interesting experience over the last week, which I interpreted in a different way than I perhaps would have a few years ago, and that interpretation is in large part due to engaging with you work.

I want to relate what I experienced and ask what (if anything) to do about it.

I was visiting some friends who have two small children (Boy [4] and Girl [6]). One evening I was brushing my teeth with Boy, and he asked me what my mom's name is. Here's roughly how I remember the conversation that followed:

Me: My mom's name is Lynn.
Boy: Oh yea, Lynn. She has an old house and a new house, and lives in the new house but not in the old house.
Me: Yea.
Boy: And you haven't been to the new house.
Me: I've been to the new house but it wasn't finished yet.
Boy: Yea, so you haven't been to the new house.
Me: Yea.
Boy: And you don't live in the old house.
Me: I lived in the old house.
Boy: You live in the old house? (incredulously)
Me: I lived in the old house when I was younger.
Boy: Oh yea.

Previously I might have brushed aside what he was saying, or thought it just a coincidence, but now I think differently. My parents have in fact recently moved into a house that they had built for them, and still own the old house (which I lived in from ~ ages 10-18). I visited the new house when it was still under construction, but haven't been there since it was completed. Seems to me that he was right on the money.

He also ardently insisted more than once while I was there that he has real wings, which his sister kept trying to deny and get him to say weren't real, to no avail. He was firm that they were real.

Additionally, one day he said multiple times that there had been a plane crash, and was making the sounds of the people who died in the crash. No confirmation on that one - when asked where the crash was he couldn't or wouldn't say, and we did hike to a high place that day where we could see the airport, so it may have been coincidence, but I have my doubts.

It looks to me like he is sensing something outside of what most modern Americans would call "real", in the materialist sense, and I am both curious as to what exactly it might be, and also about what might be done to encourage his capabilities. These friends are rather distant (~9 hours drive) and I only see them for about a week a year, so I don't imagine there is that much I can do, but his parents have some openness to magical ideas and might be willing to do things to support him if I brought it up tactfully.

In summary, my questions are:
1. What kind of extra-material perception is this evidence of, if any?
2. What might be done to encourage these perceptions safely?

Thanks for hosting this space for questions like this, I don't know where else I would go if this wasn't here!

replies.title: Re: Possible Psychism in Child
replies.content: Most children have some degree of psychism, and many of them remember at least scraps of their last incarnation. Most parents in modern American society browbeat them into suppressing those perceptions and memories. All you have to do is be supportive and listen; the perceptions will fade somewhat as the child buckles down to mastering the material world, but they'll come back later in life when it's time.

comments.title: LRM + Prayers to Greek Gods
comments.content:
Some questions for our host and possibly some of the regulars here:

1. I’ve recently started Learning Ritual Magic. I would also like to create an alter and start praying to Hermes. I believe he has reached out to me or sort of popped up at some interesting moments. And for reasons I won’t bore everyone with, he fits where I’m at in life at the moment. Would this count as the “mixing of two systems” that you’re not supposed to do? I feel like it wouldn’t interfere or mess with my work in Learning Ritual Magic but I thought I’d check first.

2. If it is ok, how do I pray to him? I know on this forum you’ve said that you simply talk to the Gods as if to a venerated elder. Is that really all? I believe I should learn the correct orphic hymn maybe? Are there little offerings or dedications I can do?

3. I would also like to pray to another deity, a deity who can help me with things like willpower, decision-making, sticking to things, mental clarity... I don’t see a Greek god that fits neatly. I’m attracted to Athena – she is a goddess of wisdom among other things, and seems to have a very forthright, no nonsense manner, and I could do with a good dose of that. I also feel like there is a good balance of powers and character between Athena and Hermes. Does this sound like a good idea? Are there other members of the Greek pantheon that might work better in that sense?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: LRM + Prayers to Greek Gods
replies.content:
1) The rule is not to mix two magical systems. Religious worship isn't magic, so you can certainly do that and a system of magical training.

2) The classic way to do it is to get a statue of the god, and then every day burn a little incense as an offering (frankincense used to be standard), recite the Orphic hymn to the deity, and then sit quietly and attentively, as though listening with your mind rather than your ears. (On the off chance you've read my fiction, the ritual that Jenny Parrish does for Tsathoggua in The Weird of Hali: Kingsport is based on this.)

3) That would be a classic pairing. You can do things exactly the same way -- burn a little incense to each of them, recite their Orphic hymns, and then listen.

comments.content:
Hi, JMG- a follow-up question to last week’s, if I may: last week I asked about your reference to the golden dawn equinox ritual helping to bring about blessing and healing to one’s neighbors/community, and you wrote the following: “If you pick up a copy of my book Circles of Power, that'll give you the ritual knowledge you need, and the text includes a solo version of the Equinox Ritual with extensive notes. It will take hard work to be ready for the fall equinox, but you can certainly be ready by spring.” This brought to mind something I’d been wondering about and meaning to ask in any case: you’ve written that the your books on golden dawn style magic gomin order of increasing complexity from LRM to Paths of Wisdom and finally Circles of Power. I haven’t much looked at Circles of Power yet, but my impression from things I’ve read here is that it’s focused more on ritual and ceremonial, while it seems Paths of Wisdom is mostly focused on meditation and path working. I know that the meditative work outlined in Paths of Wisdom takes a number of years to complete (and I’m only a month or so in, thus far). I guess what I’m wondering is whether these books are primarily meant to be worked through sequentially, first PoW, then CoP, or whether they’re more intended to be complimentary? Would you recommend progressing to a certain point in the PoW material before picking up CoP, or would you rather suggested working through them together?

Thanks as always- I know I wouldn’t be doing this work without an opportunity like this to ask questions of a teacher, and I’m genuinely grateful for it!
-NPM

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: They're complimentary. You can do Paths and Circles simultaneously -- use Paths as the source for your meditations, and Circles as the source for your ritual work.

comments.title: First time commenter: Astral Travel!
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
this is my first time posting here, thank you for hosting, it has been so nourishing for me to read the wonderful conversations happening here. I’m reading “Circles of Power” at the moment and very much appreciate it. Also subscribed to your mundane astrology.

I’d like to ask you about astral travel if I may!

Back in 2016, I was able to verify an astral travel experiment I did with my grandmother, who accidentally became an astral traveler after an NDE in her 20's. Her stories are pretty wild and I believe her experiences; she only just recently agreed to talk to me about it, as she’s been strongly religious (Christian) most of her life and unwilling to speak of it because it terrified her when it would happen, most often out of her control. I wanted to see if I could do it too, so we set up an experiment wherein I would try to visit her house astrally, across the country.

I was amazed that I was able to get there after just a few nights of trying. When I got there, I accurately read the time, as well as some text of a newspaper article that was left out face-up on the right arm of her couch, and I described the scene within the house in the wee hours of the morning; namely that she was awake earlier than usual but sitting up in bed watching TV because her dog had gotten sick during the night. It shocked me that all of this, every detail, was actually confirmed true when I spoke with her the next morning.

Emboldened, I attempted a couple of other astral travel experiences over the next week or so with success as well, however the third time I tried it, I had a very strong fearful feeling, right as I reached my destination (which was her kitchen; she had given me permission to try again at her house).

The feeling when I got there was almost that someone was “following me,” though I could not see them. I strongly felt that I shouldn’t be there. I felt very tense, as though there was some great danger to me that was present.

Since then, I've wondered if it really was an outside astral entity type of threat or not, because it felt like maybe some other part of me was pressing itself into my awareness, demanding that I stop trying to astral travel immediately. In either case, I abruptly disappeared from her kitchen and woke up in my body, in my bedroom, feeling afraid. Since then, I have wanted to try again, and have a couple of times but with less luck.

Is astral travel dangerous? Perhaps I was just unsophisticated somehow in my approach which made it more dangerous?

Or is it possible it was just my own fear of being able to do something like astral travel that reliably? I did feel afraid during that week or so, that my world view was changing rapidly and I’ve wondered if I just wasn’t ready for that.

What would you make of this? Have you written on the subject in depth somewhere I can dive into it? Would love to hear your thoughts.

I’m just in the first couple chapters of Circles of Power and started practicing the SOP this week. Mood: grateful!

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: First time commenter: Astral Travel!
replies.content: Astral travel isn't dangerous at all, and some people have a natural talent for it. You may be one of those. I haven't written on it, as I'm not one of those people, but it certainly happens and it seems to be pretty ordinary in cultures that aren't as neurotic about the spiritual worlds as ours is.

replies.title: Re: First time commenter: Astral Travel!
replies.content: Just a data point for your grandmother: there are many, many stories of Christian saints, particularly in the Orthodox church, who could do this-- it tends to be referred to as bilocation and clairvoyance, rather than astral travel. So at least in the eastern tradition it's not incompatible with Christianity, if that's any comfort to her.
